The Event Log now has a Message Details field that shows the primary key of each message created during SOAP service writer operations. This makes it easy to trace log entries directly back to their corresponding data records.

This enhancement brings several key advantages:

  • End-to-end traceability — Every message ID in the Event Log is now directly linked to its source record through the UUID shown in the Message Details field.
  • Precise record lookup — When processing large volumes of data, you can quickly pinpoint specific rows or documents to their corresponding Event Log entries without having to cross-reference manually.
  • Reliable auditability — The primary key in Message Details gives you a solid reference point for tracking data throughout SOAP service writer operations.

When a SOAP service writer processes large datasets, the Message Details field makes it easy to match any Event Log entry to its exact source record, speeding up data validation and investigation.

Data Screen

Improved Data Export

The Export All Data feature has been improved to handle exporting complete datasets, no matter how large. You can now export all available records directly from the application — whether that's thousands or hundreds of thousands of entries — giving you more flexibility and easier access to your data.

Here's what's new with this improvement:

  • Full dataset export — the Export All Data option now processes your complete dataset. 
  • CSV files are now provided in a compressed (zipped) format to reduce file size, making downloads faster. 
  • XLSX format exports are provided as standard .xlsx files. 
  • Export performance is optimized for large datasets. 

 To export all data using the Export All Data option: 

  1. Navigate to the Data Screen and open the grid view containing the data you want to export. 
  2. Choose the Export All Data option from the grid menu. 
  3. A progress indicator is displayed while the file is being generated. 
  4. Once complete, the exported file is available for download. CSV exports are provided as a zipped archive; XLSXexports are provided as standard .xlsx files. 

Joule

Extended Capabilities (File, Image, Web Search, App Help) 

Joule just got a lot smarter when it comes to understanding context. With four new input capabilities — file uploads, image and vision support, web search, and built-in application help — you can now bring external information directly into your Joule conversation and get more accurate, relevant answers.

You can now attach documents to your Joule conversation and ask questions directly about their content. Choose the paperclip icon in the message input to attach a file. Supported formats include PDF, DOCX, JPEG, PNG, and WEBP.

Once a file is uploaded, Joule can search through its content and pull out the most relevant passages. The results appear as citations in the Sources panel, complete with the filename and page reference so you always know exactly where the information came from.

Files from the recent messages remain searchable in follow-up messages. 

Joule can now see and interpret images that you paste or drop directly into the chat. Simply drag and drop or paste an image into the message input — you'll see a thumbnail preview before sending. Supported formats include JPEG, PNG, and WEBP.

Joule can describe and reason about visual content — for example, you can share a screenshot of a report or chart and ask Joule to explain what it shows. You can include up to 5 images per message.

When configured for the tenant, Joule gains access to live web results — enabling answers that go beyond internal PaPM data. You will know web search is available by the globe icon that appears in the input footer.

Turn on Web Search to allow Joule to retrieve live web results – useful for questions about recent releases, external documentation, or general SAP topics. Web search results appear as content citations with title and URL (opens in new tab). 

Note: If web search is not configured for the tenant, the globe icon is not visible.

Joule now comes with built-in PaPM domain knowledge, available in both Ask Mode and Act Mode, so it can answer conceptual and how-to questions without needing to query live data. You can ask about available function types, how allocation works, team roles and permissions, or any other PaPM concept — and Joule will give you an answer based on its built-in application knowledge. Responses from application help appear as content citations in the Sources panel.

With these four new capabilities, Joule becomes an even more powerful context-aware assistant. Whether you need to analyze an uploaded document, interpret a chart, catch up on the latest product news, or get a quick explanation of a PaPM concept — it's all available within a single conversation.

More details are available in the SAP Help Portal.  

Multi-Step Workflows (Act Mode Skills) 

Running complex PaPM operations just got a lot easier. With the introduction of Act Mode Skills, Joule can now guide you through multi-step workflows automatically — all you need to do is describe what you want in plain language.

Behind the scenes, Joule follows built-in step-by-step recipes called "skills" and runs all the required operations in the right order. You see the result — Joule handles the complexity.

In Act Mode, you can perform the following actions on the Available Workflow Categories:

Report Workflows

  • Create a full report:  including pages, page elements, and report tabs — by describing the goal in a single message. Joule handles the entire creation sequence and activates the report automatically. 
  • Add a page to a report: you can extend an existing report by adding new pages without opening the report editor. 

Process Workflows

  • Create a process: you can create a process with multiple activities and the links between them by describing the desired structure. Joule sets up all components in the correct order. 

  • Activate a process: you can run a full end-to-end process — Joule activates the process, runs all activities, handles the confirm/reject loop, and marks the process as complete. 

Environment & Function Workflows 

  • Activate an environment: Joule verifies the required functions, initiates the activation, and confirms the status — without you needing to navigate to the environment screen. 

  • Deactivate an environment: Joule asks which cleanup options to apply and then proceeds with deactivation based on your choice. 
  • Create a function: You can create and configure functions of various types – such as allocation, model entity, or calculation – in a specific environment through a single natural language request.  

  • Run a function: you can run a function and receive a status update directly in the chat.

Team Workflow 

  • Create a team: you can create a new team for a specific environment and optionally assign reader or writer access — all in one step.

Act Mode Skills are a major step forward for PaPM productivity.  Complex PaPM operations can now be completed with a single, plain language request — Joule takes care of the rest.

For more details, visit the SAP Help Portal.

Act Mode & Safety Controls 

Joule in SAP PaPM now supports Act Mode — a read-write mode that lets you create, update, and delete PaPM entities directly through natural language directly through natural language, from a single conversational user interface. Act Mode makes it easy to perform efficient and controlled PaPM operations straight from the chat.

With the new Ask/Act toggle in the Joule panel, switching to Act Mode and performing write operations is as simple as flipping a switch.

In Act Mode, users can: 

  • Create entities: users can create new PaPM entities such as environments, functions, processes, or teams by describing them in natural language. 
  • Update entities: users can modify the properties of existing entities — such as names, descriptions, or configurations — without opening the entity screen. 
  • Delete entities: users can remove entities they no longer need directly through the chat. 
  • Initiate actions: users can initiate operations such as activating or deactivating environments and running functions directly through chat. 
  • All read operations from Ask Mode also work in Act Mode. 

 To protect you from accidental data loss, Joule now includes configurable confirmation prompts for destructive and sensitive operations.

Auto Approval Toggle 

You can find this option in the Joule panel by choosing the Settings icon. By default, it's turned on for new users, meaning all operations run without confirmation. When turned off, a confirmation dialog will appear before any sensitive or destructive operation is carried out.

Note: Certain irreversible operations – such as delete, erase data, remove environment, and anonymize user – always require confirmation, regardless of the Auto Approval setting. 

Confirmation Dialog 

The confirmation dialog shows the operation type, entity name, and a warning message. When applicable, it also includes a Verify link that lets you navigate to the entity screen before making a decision. From there, you can either Approve to complete the operation or Decline to cancel it.

The confirmation dialog only appears for sensitive operations — not for simple create or update actions. This includes operations such as delete, activate, deactivate, run, erase data, erase rules, remove environment, lock/unlock data, abort/reset/complete process, confirm/reject activity, and anonymize user, among others.

When the system associates an event log with an operation, an Event Logs button appears directly in the Joule chat response. Choosing it opens the Manage Event Logs screen in a new tab, already filtered to the relevant log entry — making it easy to audit and trace every AI-driven change.

Note: The Event Logs button appears only when the system associates an event log with the operation. Not all operations generate event logs. 

For more details, visit the SAP Help Portal.

Want to catch up on previous updates? Browse our earlier What's New blog posts under the #PaPMCloudUMWhatsNew tag to see what you may have missed.

That’s all for this edition. See you in the next What's New blog post!

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model - Introducing Dynamic Packaging Type in Partitioning
entry.content:
Hello, SAP Community members! 

If you have worked with the Partitioning in SAP Profitability and Performance Management On-Premise, you already know how powerful it is. The ability to split a large dataset into ranges and run your processing functions package by package is, in many models, the difference between a job that completes in minutes and one that ties up your system for hours. The idea is elegant: define your ranges once at design time, and the engine does the rest. 

For SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model customers, this raises a natural question: does the Cloud Partitioning give me the same control I had On-Premise, including the ability to determine ranges dynamically at runtime rather than locking them in at design time? With the introduction of Dynamic Packaging Type, the answer is yes. 

The Dynamic Packaging Type in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model follows the same principle as its On-Premise counterpart: ranges are no longer pre-defined as inner nodes but are determined at runtime from the input data of the function where partitioning is applied. For background on the On-Premise partitioning and packaging setup, see the SAP Help Portal documentation.

Meet the Dynamic Packaging Type in Partitioning

The Dynamic Packaging Type is found in the Partitioning within the General Entities. This is a new packaging option that brings a fundamentally different approach to how processing operations are split across data ranges at runtime. Until now, only Static packaging was available in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model; you define your ranges as inner nodes at design time, and those ranges are fixed for every run. 

One key structural difference worth knowing upfront: with Dynamic Packaging, you no longer define range nodes inside the Partitioning at all. Any range nodes previously defined inside a Static Packaging are removed when you switch to Dynamic. The ranges are built fresh at runtime, derived from the input function entries of the function where dynamic partitioning is applied.

Configuring the Partitioning: Packaging Type

Expand the Partitioning in your model. The Properties panel now includes the new field placed directly after Run Mode. 

Packaging Type: Determining How Ranges Are Defined 

This is the central new field introduced by this enhancement. 

Packaging Type (dropdown, mandatory): Determines whether ranges are defined at design time or derived at runtime. 

The dropdown offers two values: 

  • Static 
  • Dynamic 

Static preserves the existing behavior — ranges are defined as inner nodes inside the Partitioning at design time, exactly as before. 

Dynamic is the new Packaging Type introduced by this enhancement. Unlike Static, where ranges are locked in at design time, Dynamic determines partition ranges at runtime — derived automatically from the input function entries of the function where dynamic partitioning is applied. Note that switching an existing Partitioning from Static to Dynamic removes any range nodes previously defined inside it. 

The default value for Packaging Type is Static. Existing Partitioning built without this field are treated as Static automatically. No migration of existing configurations is required. 

Packaging Field: Defining the Field for Dynamic Range Creation 

Another mandatory companion to Packaging Type, present for both Static and Dynamic configurations. 

Packaging Field (dropdown, mandatory): Defines which field values are used to create the partition ranges at runtime.

Note: The field previously labeled Field in the Partitioning has been renamed to Packaging Field. This rename applies to all Partitioning, both Static and Dynamic configurations. 

Before You Begin

Before configuring a Dynamic Packaging Type, keep the following constraints in mind: 

  • Range Levels and Destinations are not supported for Dynamic Packaging Type. These features are only available with Static packaging. 
  • For Dynamic Packaging Type, the following Run Mode values are not displayed in the dropdown when Dynamic is selected: PPBP, PPDP, SPXP, SUXP. 
  • For a given Packaging Field, you can define multiple Partitioning as long as each has a different Run Mode or Packaging Type setting.

How It Works

To help illustrate, here is a complete end-to-end walkthrough of configuring and running a Partitioning with Dynamic Packaging Type. 

  1. Open your model in the Modeling environment and locate the Partitioning you want to configure (or create a new one from the Palette). 
  2. Open the Properties panel. Locate the Packaging Type field, placed directly after Run Mode. 
  3. Set Packaging Type to Dynamic.  
  4. Set Packaging Field to the field whose values will drive range creation at runtime. Confirm that this field exists as an input to the function where dynamic partitioning will be applied. 
    Note that PPBP, PPDP, SPXP, and SUXP Run Mode values will not be displayed in the Run Mode dropdown when Dynamic Packaging Type is selected.
  5. Save the Partitioning configuration. 
  6. Execute your process. At runtime, the system reads the distinct values of the Packaging Field from the input function entries and constructs the partition ranges dynamically before processing begins. 
  7. Check the Application Monitor to confirm the run completed and to see how many package instances were created. In our example, if the input function contains entries for four customers, the engine creates four partition ranges at runtime and processes each as a separate package instance. 

This is the complete end-to-end flow: the Packaging Type field drives whether ranges come from design-time nodes or runtime data, the Packaging Field identifies the dimension that structures those runtime ranges, and the engine handles range creation automatically before execution begins. See SAP Help related to Partitioning and Range Setup for further details. 

Wrapping Up

The Dynamic Packaging Type removes a configuration step that, for many models, was also a maintenance burden: keeping design-time range nodes synchronized with the actual data in your system. Whether you work with a cost center hierarchy that changes frequently and you want packaging to always reflect the current structure, a profit center landscape that varies by version and requires a different set of ranges for each scenario, or a model where the right partition boundaries simply cannot be known until the data arrives at runtime, Dynamic Packaging Type gives you a clean, supported way to let the data define its own partitions. 

We are eager to hear from you. Which datasets in your models are you thinking of migrating from Static to Dynamic packaging? Have you encountered situations where design-time range maintenance was a limiting factor in your partitioning strategy? Share your thoughts in the comments below and stay tuned for further enhancements to the Partitioning in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model. 

As always, feel free to reach out via the SAP Profitability and Performance Management Community if you have questions. 

Related Resources 

  • Partitioning and Range Setup - SAP Help Portal (SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model) 
  • Different Run Modes - SAP Help Portal (SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model) 
  • Partitioning and Packaging Setup - SAP Profitability and Performance Management On-Premise

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model: What's New as of 2026-07-06?
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

Welcome to this month's What's New blog post for SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model (SAP PaPM Cloud SM). We're delighted to present the latest improvements and innovations that continue to enhance the platform, helping you work more efficiently while delivering a smoother and more intuitive experience.

Let's explore what's new! 🚀

Process Scheduler | Environment Lifecycle Management

Previously, recurring Process Scheduler jobs were not part of the environment lifecycle. Every environment copy or import meant recreating all job definitions manually in the target environment, and deleting an environment left orphaned jobs behind in the Process Scheduler.

With this update, recurring Process Scheduler jobs are now fully integrated across four environment operations: Copy, Export, Import, and Deletion. Job definitions travel with their environment and are automatically removed when the environment is deleted. Copy and import operations include an opt-in checkbox to include recurring jobs, and all operations are tracked in the Application Monitor.

COPY:

IMPORT:

For a full walkthrough of each operation, see our dedicated blog post. For more information, refer to the Process Scheduler documentation on the SAP Help Portal.

Content Management and Application Monitor | Improved Logs for Environment Import

We've improved the logging for environment imports in Content Management. The import log entry now includes the original imported file name, giving you better visibility and traceability during the import process.

What changed?

Previously, when an environment import was initiated, the Application Monitor log showed:

"Model Import started for Environment=DSE, Version=0010"

With this improvement, the log now displays: 

"Model Import started for Environment=DSE, Version=0010 based on the original content DSE-0001-20260527T132918847Z.zip" 



Why does this matter? 

When managing multiple imports — especially in automated TMS (Transport Management System) deployments — it was difficult to trace back which specific file initiated a particular import. The updated log entry now makes it easy to:

  1. Identify the exact source file of any import.
  2. Audit and troubleshoot import activities more efficiently.
  3. Correlate Application Monitor entries with the files used in your deployment pipeline.

Removal of the Optional Feature for the Python Executor

The Optional Feature for the Python Executor has been removed from the Settings screen. The Python 3.13 implementation is now the permanent and only supported executor for the Remote Function Adapter (RFA) Python function type in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model (SAP PaPM Cloud SM).

Previously, administrators could activate  the new Python executor and switch back to the previous implementation if required. As the migration to Python 3.13 has been completed, this configuration option is no longer available, and all RFA Python functions now run using the latest implementation by default.

What Changed?

  • The use New Remote Function Adapter Python Implementation toggle has been removed from Administration > Settings > Optional Features.
  • Python 3.13 is now the permanent and only supported Python executor.
  • Administrators no longer need to configure the Python executor version.

Benefits:

  • Simplifies system administration by removing an obsolete configuration option.
  • Ensures a consistent Python execution environment across all tenants.
  • Eliminates the possibility of reverting to the legacy Python executor, providing a single supported runtime for all RFA Python executions.

Modeling | Partitioning Function: Dynamic Packaging Type

The Partitioning function now supports a new Packaging Type dropdown, added directly after the Run Mode field in the properties panel. This lets you choose whether partition ranges are fixed at design time or calculated at runtime from the actual Input function data.

  • Static (default): Ranges are defined at design time as inner nodes in the Partitioning function. No change to existing models are required.
  • Dynamic: Ranges are calculated at runtime from the Input function entries. Any existing Range nodes are removed when a Static Partitioning is updated to Dynamic.

When Dynamic is selected, Run Mode values PPBP, PPDP, SPXP, and SUXP are not available, and Range Levels and Destinations are not supported.



The Field property has been renamed to Packaging Field and repositioned directly after Packaging Type. Multiple Partitioning functions can reference the same Packaging Field as long as each uses a different Run Mode or Packaging Type.


For more details about the Dynamic Packaging Partitioning, you may check SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model - Introducing Dynamic Packaging Type in Partitioning.

Joule | AI Enhancements

Joule now supports uploading .txt and .docx files as additional context for your conversations, expanding the range of file formats available for upload.


Report Management | Create, Rewrite, or Delete Report Sections

You can now use Joule in Act mode to create new text sections, rewrite existing ones, or delete sections from your report directly through the Joule conversation panel — without manually editing the report.

Previously, report text could only be modified by entering Edit mode manually. The Inline Report Editing feature was introduced, allowing you to rewrite or improve text directly within your report.

With this update, you can instruct Joule conversationally to shape your report content for a single section, or multiple sections in a single prompt.

To get started, open the Joule panel in the Report Management application, choose Act to switch to Act mode, and type your instruction referring to the target section by its title.

The following actions are supported:

Action

Example Prompt

Rewrite an existing section    

"Please rewrite the Processes and Activities section giving it more of the business touch."

Add a new text section

"Add a new Key Risks and Mitigations section after the Summary section."

Delete a text section

"Delete the Introduction section."

Joule applies non-destructive actions (create, rewrite) immediately and automatically refreshes the report screen — the requested modification is applied and displayed in the report without any manual refresh needed.

For destructive actions such as deletion, Joule asks for your confirmation before proceeding — choose Approve to apply or Reject to cancel.



The following notes apply:

  • If you add a new section without specifying a position, Joule adds it at the end of the report.
  • Deleted sections cannot be recovered.
  • For rewritten sections, you can ask Joule to restore the previous content based on the conversation context.
  • If you are in Edit mode with unsaved changes, save or discard them first — Joule will prompt you if needed.

For more information, refer to the relevant pages on our SAP Help Portal: Create, Rewrite, or Delete Report Sections | Prompting Recommendations | Inline Report Editing

Modeling Screen | Remote Function Adapter: General Function

A new function type is now available in the Remote Function Adapter (RFA) palette in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model: the General Function.


The General Function connects SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model to a custom Function Module on your On-Premise SAP system through an RFC destination configured in SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). This allows existing On-Premise ABAP logic — written as a BAPI wrapper — to be called directly from your calculation model, with input data passed in through IT_DATA and results returned through ET_DATA into the result table. No rebuilding of On-Premise logic in the Cloud is required.

To use the General Function, the following must be in place:

  1. A custom Function Module on your On-Premise system, configured as Remote-Enabled (SE37) and structured with the required IT_DATA, ET_DATA, and ET_MESSAGE parameters.
  2. An RFC Destination in SAP BTP Destination Service, with the On-Premise system mapped via SAP Cloud Connector.



Once configured, the General Function can be run through all standard SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model run entry points, including direct Run, Run with Parameters and Selections, Process Template, Process Instance, and Process Scheduler.

For a full walkthrough of configuration, BAPI compatibility requirements, and end-to-end verification, see our dedicated blog post: SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model – Introducing the Remote Function Adapter: General Function.

File Uploads | Enhanced File Validation Across Applications

File upload and import operations across SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model now include enhanced security validations, providing stronger protection against malformed, misnamed, or potentially malicious files.

Previously, uploads were protected by malware scanning only. With this update, additional validation checks are applied automatically during every file upload or import operation including real file type detection (verifying the actual file content, not just the extension), file name validation, and improved handling of archive files.

The following applications are affected:

Application

Use Case

Supported Formats

Data Management

Upload data to Model Tables and Queries

CSV, XLSX

Content Management

Upload / import environments

ZIP

Process Management

Mass upload of process instance settings

XLSX

Report Management

Upload images to Report panels

BMP, JPEG, JPG, GIF, PNG, TIFF, WEBP

Joule

Attach files to Joule conversations

CSV, XLSX, PDF

No action is required on your side as the validations run automatically. Each step of the file upload process is logged, and the system displays an appropriate message to inform you of the outcome whether the upload was successful, or the file was rejected due to a validation or security scanning failure.

For more information, refer to our SAP Help Portal: Secure File Handling  

That's all for this update!

We hope these enhancements help further improve your experience with SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model. Stay tuned for more updates and innovations in the coming months.

Until next time! 😊

entry.title: Joule in SAP PaPM UM Cloud: Light or Intensive - Choose Right Before You Switch It On
entry.content:
AI gets all its applause for what it can do.

Answer questions, explain an untraceable number, guide a user through a process, and remove an hour of manual work.

But none of that happens in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Universal Model Cloud the moment Joule shows up on the screen. It happens when one quieter thing is true first: the connection is set up correctly.

Here’s the part most teams skip past - there isn’t one way to connect Joule. There are two. And picking the wrong one for how you actually plan to use AI is the whole difference between “it just works” and “why is it so slow, why did that fail, why can’t I get in?”

So before you switch anything on, answer one honest question: Are we using AI lightly, or are we about to lean on it every single day?

Let’s walk both paths.

Path 1: Light Usage - It’s Already In the Box

Here’s the good news that saves a lot of effort: SAP Profitability and Performance Management Universal Model Cloud already ships with built-in AI, ready to use through a preinstalled Joule client. No dedicated AI Core, no model to deploy, no destination to wire. It’s already there - you don’t build the connection, you just start asking.

There’s one rule you have to respect, and it’s there by design. A Fair Use Policy applies per day, per tenant. Stay inside light usage and everything runs smoothly. Push past it - too many requests, too fast - and automatic throttling steps in to protect the shared capacity.

Think of it as a shared lane on a highway. Perfectly quick when traffic is normal, but it isn’t your private road. Flood it, and the system deliberately slows you to keep things fair for everyone else on the tenant. That single fact is what makes light usage perfect for occasional AI - and the wrong fit for heavy, all-day work.

To be clear, light usage isn't a preview - it's the full experience. Here are some assistants you'll find across the product:

  1. Report and Simulation Assistant: Adjust a what-if scenario inside a report (set the What-If percentage for Account Training to 90 and watch the plan-and-forecast update).
  2. Process and Activities Assistant: Drive processes by conversation-activate a process for an environment, confirm it's live, and see how many activities it carries.
  3. Analysis Assistant (in the Show screen): Ask plain-language questions about the data ("what's the total for this GL account this fiscal year?") and get precise, context-aware answers.
  4. Model Assistant: Build your model by describing it - create environments, functions, fields, and more, without clicking through every screen.
  5. Knowledge Assistant: Ask how things work and get answers grounded in product knowledge, so guidance is right where you're working.
  6. Connection Assistant: Set up and check your connections conversationally, so data sources and integrations come together with less manual effort.

These are a few examples: Joule appears in more places, and its capabilities keep expanding.

If you use light, the entire “setup” comes down to three things:

  1. Confirm your subscription is active and that the Joule client is available.
  2. Ensure that you are assigned the required roles.
  3. Enter your request in Joule and proceed with your task.

If that's you, you can stop reading here - you're done, and you didn't build a thing.

Path 2: Intensive Usage - Bring Your Own Engine

But what if AI isn’t an occasional helper? What if it’s becoming part of daily operations - many users, many questions, all day? Then the shared lane stops being enough, and the guidance is blunt about it: for intensive usage, set up and connect to a dedicated SAP AI Core to ensure high speed. This is where you stop borrowing capacity and start running your own engine - more headroom, better performance, more control.

Heads-up on regions. Create and use your AI Core instance in the same region as your current PaPM instance - and only where AI Core is actually supported. The supported regions today are ap10, ap11, ap20, ap30, br10, eu10, eu11, eu20, eu30, in30 jp10, us10, us21, and us30. Confirm your PaPM region is on this list before you start.

Once it’s in place, the rest is one clean flow through four stages - and the order is the whole game:

 

① Build the engine - SAP AI Core

The first stage stands up the engine that actually runs the models, and it ends with the single most important artifact in this whole process: a service key. Make sure you’re on the AI Core Extended plan here - that’s the one that unlocks generative AI.

Here’s how to set it up:

  1. Create a subaccount in SAP BTP (You can use a different subaccount from the one used for SAP Profitability and Performance Management.)
  2. Enable the Cloud Foundry environment in the subaccount.
  3. Create a space within the Cloud Foundry environment.
  4. Add the required service plan for SAP AI Core.
  5. Create a service instance of SAP AI Core.
  6. Create and download the service key for the service instance.

Treat that key like the crown jewels. The URL, client ID, and client secret inside it are exactly what you’ll paste into the destination two stages from now. Everything downstream reads from this one file - save it somewhere safe and don’t lose it.

See detailed setup steps : Build the engine - SAP AI Core 

② Deploy the model - SAP AI Launchpad

A model you’re entitled to isn’t automatically up and running. Stage two closes that gap. Start by choosing the AI Launchpad Standard plan, then set it up just like you did with SAP AI Core - create a subaccount, set up Cloud Foundry, and add the service plan. From there, subscribe to the service, assign yourself the necessary access, and launch it.

Now the real work, and it's a short sequence. First, add a connection to SAP AI Core using the service key from Stage ①. Then create a configuration: give it a name, choose the foundation-models scenario, and pick the model you want to serve.

From that configuration, create a deployment - the live endpoint that will actually answer requests.

If this is your very first Launchpad, you'll also need to create the supported embedding model, text-embedding-ada-002 - the integration depends on it.

And then you wait for one word. Open the deployment and watch its status until it reads “Running.” Need more than one model? Just repeat the configure-and-deploy loop for each.

This is the gate for everything that follows. If the deployment isn’t “Running,” the destination you build next has nothing on the other end - and Joule will look connected while every question quietly vanishes.

See detailed setup steps : Deploy the model - SAP AI Launchpad 

③ Build the bridge - the BTP destination

With a running model, stage three connects PaPM to it through an HTTP destination in the SAP BTP Cockpit. You’ll head into your PaPM subaccount, open Connectivity → Destinations, and create a new one from scratch.

This is the one place where the exact values matter, because a typo here is invisible until Joule silently fails - so slow down. And here’s the detail you can’t get wrong: the name is not free form. For Joule in the Universal Model, the destination must use the predefined name PAPM_UM_AI_UNIVERSAL. Get a single character wrong and the integration won’t find it.

With the name set, the rest is transcription. Set the type to HTTP, proxy type to Internet, and authentication to OAuth2ClientCredentials. The URL is your service key’s API URL with /v2/inference on the end; the Client ID and Client Secret come straight out of that same service key; and the Token Service URL is the key’s URL with /oauth/token appended, using a Dedicated token service type. Leave the two mTLS / basic-credentials checkboxes unticked.

Then comes the part that actually decides how Joule behaves - the Additional properties. This is where you choose the models, so it’s worth getting right. There are four:

configuration Parameters

 
Parameter

Requirement

Description

ai-resource-group

Required

Your AI Launchpad workspace’s resource group. Often just default.

ai_modelRequiredThe primary model Joule reasons with. Recommended: anthropic--claude-4.6-sonnet, claude-4.6-opus, claude-4.5-sonnet, or claude-4.5-opus.
websearch_modelOptionalSet it to sonar or sonar-pro to let Joule pull real-time web search into its answers.
ai_support_modelOptionalA lighter secondary model (anthropic--claude-4.5-haiku or gemini-2.5-flash) that handles the simpler tasks alongside the primary one.
 

Fill those in, then save.

Every one of those service-key values is sitting in the file you downloaded in Stage ①. This stage is really just transcribing that key carefully into the right boxes - under the one name the integration expects - and choosing which models do the thinking.

See detailed setup steps : Build the bridge - the BTP destination 

④ It’s already live - tenant-wide

Here’s the quietly satisfying part: there’s no final “now point PaPM at it” step to fumble. Because the system finds the connection by its fixed name, the moment you save PAPM_UM_AI_UNIVERSAL, it applies across every environment in the tenant. No per-environment wiring, no name to copy into a settings screen, nothing left to forget.

That’s the whole handshake. The engine is running, the model is deployed and “Running,” and the destination is in place under the name Joule’s Universal Agent goes looking for. Joule goes live - only now it’s drawing on your own dedicated AI Core. Same assistant your users see; far more room to breathe behind it.

A Word on Capacity - Size It Before You Sign
That €10k minimum buys Capacity Units, and how far they stretch depends entirely on which model you run and how hard you run it.

The lesson hiding in that trade-off is simple - capacity is a function of the model. A chatty model with long answers burns far more than a lean one. So don’t guess. SAP’s Generative AI hub Cost Calculator lets you plug in your model, your token sizes, and your monthly volume and tells you what you’ll actually consume. Run that before you finalize the BTPEA, not after the invoice surprises you.

How to Choose - In One Breath

If your AI use is occasional, stay light: it’s already built in, you respect the daily Fair Use Policy, you accept that heavy bursts get throttled, and you start today without building anything.

If AI is becoming part of daily business, go intensive: confirm your region is supported, get the BTPEA in place, then walk the four stages in order - engine, model, bridge, live - naming the destination exactly PAPM_UM_AI_UNIVERSAL and choosing your models in the destination’s properties, and size your Capacity Units against the model you actually intend to use.

The real mistake isn’t choosing one over the other. It’s not choosing - drifting into heavy use on the light model and wondering why it throttles, or paying for a dedicated engine you don’t yet need.

Final Thought

The best AI experiences feel effortless to the person using them. They almost never get that way by accident.

Behind a smooth Joule moment in Profitability and Performance Management Universal Model Cloud, there’s a decision someone made on purpose: light or intensive, fair-use lane or dedicated engine, the right model sized to the real workload - and, when it’s intensive, four stages walked in the right order.

Get the quiet groundwork right, and Joule stops being a button in the corner of the screen. It becomes the assistant you actually reach for.

For the complete, official step-by-step instructions, see the SAP Help Portal 

To understand Joule capability, see the Introducing the Joule in Universal Model for SAP PaPM: When Your Model Finally Talks Back..
